name: One More Than Boarded
alias: the last passenger · the commuter the carriage counts twice, who alights where nothing stops
body: It is the sound that tells you first, or rather the absence of it: the last train at this hour is never silent, there is always the buzz of the tube lights and the long sigh of the doors and the recorded voice reminding you, in four languages, to mind the platform gap, but on this train there is a stillness in one seat that the noise seems to bend around. You look up because the carriage felt fuller than it should at twenty-five past midnight, when the only other passengers are the ones who fell asleep past their stop and the cleaners riding home in their faded uniforms. You count the heads out of the same idle habit that makes you read every advertisement twice, that makes you trace the coloured line of the route map from terminus to terminus. You count them again. There is one more than boarded with you at the platform, and you did not see the doors open in between, did not hear the chime, did not feel the train slow for any station that the strip map admits exists.

The last passenger does nothing that a tired commuter would not do. It sits with a bag on its lap and its face tilted toward the dark window, and it watches the tunnel as if there were scenery out there, as if the black glass showed kampung and sea and not the concrete throat of the line. The rule the tellers give is about the stops: you must not get off where it gets off. It rises a beat before the train slows, the way only someone who knows the line by heart can, the way the night-shift people do who could ride it blind, and it leaves at a station whose name the recorded voice does not announce, a platform with the lights at half, tiled in a colour the network stopped using long ago, the cream and rust of the very first stations before everything was made bright and blue. If your stop and its stop are the same, you stay seated. You ride one further into the night, you let the doors close on the wrong platform, and you walk back along the road above, past the shuttered coffeeshops and the lone supper stall. To follow it onto that platform is to be the one extra head on tomorrow's last train.

What stays with people is not fear exactly but a kind of courtesy, the way the thing keeps the manners of a passenger to the very end, shifting its bag to make room that no one needs, even reaching up to steady itself on a handgrip the moment before the brakes catch. Those who have seen it say it always carries the same object, and here the detail is so domestic it unsettles the most: a single styrofoam packet of supper, the rubber band still on, doubled the way the stall uncle always doubles it, gone cold, the gravy long since seeped through one corner and dried to a dark rind, as though it had been bringing food home to someone for a very long time, riding the last train out past Pasir Ris and back, and had simply never arrived.
note: You hear this on the last services out of the interchanges, Jurong East, Bishan, the long run down the East West Line where the train surfaces and the windows turn to mirrors. SBS and SMRT night staff have their own quieter versions, mostly about the count on the platform CCTV never matching the count in the cabin. The student forums tell it as a warning dressed up as a dare, which is how the best of these survive.

The disputed point is always which closed station the passenger alights at; some name a halt that shut decades ago, others insist it is a platform that was sketched on an old map and never built at all, and no one agrees. The half-working ward is to keep your earphones in and your eyes on your own reflection, and above all not to count, because counting is the invitation. Probably nothing more than the brain inventing a stranger out of fatigue and an empty seat, and yet conductors still walk that final carriage twice.
